content.assessment: This is a partial structural reform, removing one of three selection points while retaining two. Applying the lens's implementation-sequencing criterion, Poland's experience demonstrates that even full single-stage consolidation reforms carry significant transition costs; a partial reform removing only the earliest entry point may reduce implementation scale relative to full consolidation but does not have a direct Polish precedent, since Poland's 1999 reform was a full structural consolidation rather than a partial, single-entry-point removal. The distributional-effects criterion is therefore only weakly informed by Polish evidence: Poland's gains are associated with full elimination of early tracking rather than partial removal, so whether a partial version reproduces proportional benefits is untested in the cited record.
opportunities: Could serve as an incremental step informed by Poland's demonstrated implementation costs, potentially avoiding the double-cohort transition crisis by limiting scope
strengths: Smaller implementation footprint than full structural consolidation reduces analogic risk of the severe disruption documented in Poland's reversal (thousands unplaced, mass school closures) [F-live-polish_reform-05]
threats: Risk that displaced selection pressure concentrates at remaining entry points, a dynamic not tested in the Polish precedent, which eliminated all early entry points simultaneously rather than sequentially removing one
weaknesses: Polish evidence pertains to full structural reform (6+3+3 to single-structure and back), not partial removal of a single entry point, limiting direct applicability to this partial-reform design
No basis in the supplied Polish evidence to estimate whether partial removal produces proportional, negligible, or displaced distributional effects
